text: The surgeon evaluates the crossover area, noting the abrupt end of the linea alba striations. The absence of the rectus muscle and anterior sheath reveals subcutaneous fat, which could be misleading. Muscle fibers are visible, possibly remnants from previous surgery, as the dissection continues near the defined medial aspect of the linea alba.

The surgeon identifies the mesh located medially and decides to approach the dissection above it to avoid the peritoneum. A vessel is identified and preserved, potentially feeding the tram flap. The surgeon finds a suitable dissection plane and maintains alignment along the linea alba.

The surgeon assesses the lateral extent of the dissection, carefully avoiding neurovascular bundles, which may have been compromised. The dissection is guided by the proximity to a previously identified vessel, ensuring the skin is not inadvertently punctured due to its close proximity.

The surgeon uses a quick peak port to drain air and gain a visual window into the abdomen, revealing a hernia defect and a very thin peritoneum. Two linear strips of mesh are visible, possibly indicating previous surgical repairs. The surgeon assesses the trajectory of the dissection and plans to reduce the hernia sac before proceeding.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the mesh and begins the transversus abdominis release (TAR) plane using kinder dissectors. The mesh is elevated with the left hand, while the dissectors help maintain tension and soak up blood, allowing clear visualization of the dissection plane. The surgeon carefully adjusts tension to avoid tears, despite some occurring due to mesh adhesion.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, navigating past troublesome areas while the mesh is visible. There is disruption of the anterior tissue planes, requiring careful evaluation to ensure correct positioning. The surgeon uses intra-abdominal views through a rent and external patient assessment to maintain spatial awareness.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon attempts to locate the epigastric vessels to guide the trajectory towards the inguinal region. Several surgical clips are visible along the base, indicating previous vessel sacrifice. The round ligament is nearby, complicating the identification of the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for hematomas, with visible pooling of fluid in the area. The assistant PA is seen preparing the mesh while the surgeon uses suction to remove excess fluid.

The surgeon places the mesh, tailored to fit the defect, and applies VistaSeal for hemostasis and space inclusion. The abdomen is insufflated, and the surgeon confirms there are no breaks in the peritoneum or posterior sheath. A laparoscopic tap block is performed circumferentially on both sides to complete the procedure.
